coredata.dc:title: Chondroitin sulfate/silk fibroin hydrogel incorporating graphene oxide quantum dots with photothermal-effect promotes type H vessel-related wound healing
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Tanjun
Lu, Wenli
Zhao, Xiaoxian
Wang, Haoyu
Zheng, Yumeng
Zheng, Ao
Shen, Zhengyu
coredata.dc:description: Chronic wounds with bacterial infection present formidable clinical challenges. In this study, a versatile hydrogel dressing with antibacterial and angiogenic activity composite of silk fibroin (SF), chondroitin sulfate (CS), and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) is fabricated. GOQDs@SF/CS (GSC) hydrogel is rapidly formed through the enzyme catalytic action of horseradish peroxidase. With the incorporation of GOQDs both gelation speed and mechanical properties have been enhanced, and the photothermal characteristics of GOQDs in GSC hydrogel enabled bacterial killing through photothermal treatment (PTT) at ∼51 °C. In vitro studies show that the GSC hydrogels demonstrate excellent antibacterial performance and induce type H vessel differentiation of endothelial cells via the activated ERK1/2 signaling pathway and upregulated SLIT3 expression. In vivo results show that the hydrogel significantly promotes type H vessels formation, which is related to the collagen deposition, epithelialization and, ultimately, accelerates the regeneration of infected skin defects. Collectively, this multifunctional GSC hydrogel, with dual action of antibacterial efficacy and angiogenesis promotion, emerges as an innovative skin dressing with the potential for advancing in infected wound healing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of the GOQDs and GSC hydrogels. A) TEM image of the GOQDs. B) 2D AFM image and corresponding liner scanning profile of the GOQDs. The white line indicates the height analysis result at a liner scanning distance of 2 μm. C) 3D AFM image of the GOQDs. D) Photographs of the GSC hydrogels before and after gelation. E) Low-resolution XPS scanning profile and F) FTIR spectrum of SF, CS, GOQDs and GSC10 hydrogel.

Morphology and physicochemical properties of the GSC hydrogels. A) SEM images (scale bars = 100 μm) and B) pore size analysis of different lyophilized GSC hydrogels. C) Water contact angle images and D) data analysis of different GSC hydrogels. E) Swelling behavior and F) degradation properties of the GSC hydrogels immersed in PBS. G) Stress-strain curves and H) Young's modulus for tensile test of different GSC hydrogels. I) Stress-strain curves and J) compression modulus for compression test of different GSC hydrogels. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), ns = not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

In vitro photothermal performance of the GSC hydrogels. A) Thermal images of different GSC hydrogels under 808 nm NIR irradiation (2.0 W cm−2, 300 s). B) Temperature rise curves of the GSC hydrogels containing different GOQDs concentrations (SC, GSC1, GSC5, GSC10) under 808 nm NIR irradiation. C) Temperature rise curves of GCS10 hydrogel with various power densities (0.6, 1.3, 2.0 and 2.7 W cm−2) under 808 nm NIR irradiation. D) Photothermal-conversion efficiency of the GSC10 hydrogel. E) Photothermal stability of the GSC10 hydrogel under repeated on-off NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2).

In vitro antibacterial capability of the GSC hydrogels. A) Optical images of E. coli and S. aureus bacterial colonies after treatments via different hydrogels with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 300 s) and B-C) the corresponding bacterial survival ratio results. D) Live/dead staining of E. coli and S. aureus after treatments via different hydrogels with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 300 s; scale bars = 50 μm) and E-F) the corresponding live/dead fluorescence ratios. G) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus after treatments via different hydrogels with NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 300 s, scale bars = 1 μm). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

In vitro biocompatibility of the GSC hydrogels. Live/dead staining of A) L929 cells and B) HUVECs cultured with different hydrogels for 1, 3 and 5 days (scale bars = 200 μm). The CCK-8 assay of C) L929 cells and D) HUVECs cultured with different hydrogels for 1, 3 and 5 days. E) The hematolysis ratio of different hydrogels and the corresponding photographs (PC: positive control, 0.1 % Triton X-100; NC: negative control, PBS). Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

In vitro promotion of vascularization. A) Transwell assay of HUVECs after treatment with different hydrogels for 12 h (scale bars = 200 μm) and B) the corresponding migrated cells. C) Scratch assay of HUVECs after treatments with different hydrogels (scale bars = 500 μm) and D-E) the corresponding percentage of scratching areas after incubation for 12 and 24 h. F) Tube formation assay of HUVECs after treatments with different hydrogel for 6 h (scale bars = 500 μm), and the corresponding G) number of junctions and H) total vessel length of tube formation assay. I-J) Relative mRNA expression of CD31 and Emcn of HUVECs after treatment with different hydrogels for 48 h. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Mechanism of GSC hydrogels on differentiation of HUVECs toward type H vessels. A) PCA analysis of the samples with or without GSC10 hydrogel treatment. B) Volcano plot of the differentially expressed genes (DEGs). C) Gene ontology (GO) enrichment results in biological process. D) Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) results based on GO. E) Western blot results of CD31, Emcn, SLIT3, ERK1/2 and p-ERK1/2 in HUVECs after treatments with different hydrogels for 48 h.

In vivo wound healing performance of the GSC10 hydrogel. A) Schematic diagram of the wound healing experiment in the infected full-thickness skin defect model. B) Representative photographs of skin wounds treated with PBS, SC, GSC10, GSC10 + NIR on day 0, 1, 3, 7 and 14. C) Traces of wound closure on day 0, 1, 3, 7 and 14. D) Wound closure ratio of different groups. E) Photographs of the survival bacterial colonies from wound sites of different treatment groups on day 3. F) Bacterial survival ratio of different groups. G) Body weights monitoring from day 0 to day 14. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), ns = not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Histological evaluation of the skin tissues after treatment with different hydrogel. A) H&E staining on day 7 and 14 (scale bars = 1 mm and 400 μm). B-C) Quantitative analysis of wound width on day 7 and 14. D) Masson's trichrome staining on day 14 (scale bars = 1 mm and 400 μm). E) Quantitative analysis of Masson's trichrome staining on day 14. F) TNF-α and IL-6 staining on day 7 and G-H) the corresponding quantitative analysis. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Promotion of type H vessel formation in vivo. A) Immunostaining of CD31 and Emcn on day 7 (scale bars = 200 μm). B-D) Relative mRNA expression of CD31, Emcn and SLIT3 of skin tissues on day 7. E) Western blot results of CD31, Emcn, SLIT3, ERK1/2 and p-ERK1/2 of skin tissues on day 7. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 3), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.
Schematic illustration for the design strategy and application of GOQDs@silk fibroin/chondroitin sulfate (GSC) hydrogel as a multifunctional dressing for bacteria-infected skin wound healing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chronic wounds with bacterial infection present formidable clinical challenges. In this study, a versatile hydrogel dressing with antibacterial and angiogenic activity composite of silk fibroin (SF), chondroitin sulfate (CS), and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) is fabricated. GOQDs@SF/CS (GSC) hydrogel is rapidly formed through the enzyme catalytic action of horseradish peroxidase. With the incorporation of GOQDs both gelation speed and mechanical properties have been enhanced, and the photothermal characteristics of GOQDs in GSC hydrogel enabled bacterial killing through photothermal treatment (PTT) at ∼51 °C. In vitro studies show that the GSC hydrogels demonstrate excellent antibacterial performance and induce type H vessel differentiation of endothelial cells via the activated ERK1/2 signaling pathway and upregulated SLIT3 expression. In vivo results show that the hydrogel significantly promotes type H vessels formation, which is related to the collagen deposition, epithelialization and, ultimately, accelerates the regeneration of infected skin defects. Collectively, this multifunctional GSC hydrogel, with dual action of antibacterial efficacy and angiogenesis promotion, emerges as an innovative skin dressing with the potential for advancing in infected wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the largest organ covering the whole body, skin is particularly vulnerable to various external hazards and often forms irregular wound defects which will easily lead to bacterial infections, resulting in a prolonged healing process and potentially dire consequences in chronic wound management (Kalelkar et al., 2022). Thus, considerable efforts have been taken to explore more efficient anti-infection therapies to prevent chronic wounds (Puthia et al., 2020). Wound healing is a complex process that unfolds through four interrelated phases: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation and remodeling. Angiogenesis, a crucial process in the proliferation phase, is closely associated with epithelium regeneration and remodeling during wound healing (Yuge et al., 2023). Neovascularization can provide damaged tissues with the necessary nutrients and oxygen, thereby enhancing the efficiency and quality of wound healing (Han et al., 2023). Recently, the identification of type H vessels in skin tissues, characterized by high expression of both platelet endothelial cell adhesion molecule-1 (CD31) and endomucin (Emcn), has revealed their significant role in skin wound healing under both physiological and pathological conditions (Lin et al., 2023). This realization has prompted the pursuit of novel therapeutic approaches aimed at fostering type H vessel formation to expedite the wound regeneration process. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop skin dressings with antibacterial and angiogenic properties that can effectively combat bacterial infections without engendering drug resistance while simultaneously promoting the repair and regeneration of infected skin defects.

Compared with traditional methods like sutures, gauze, and bandages, hydrogel with the similar component of extracellular matrix (ECM) has the advantages of high water content and three-dimensional spatial structure. Hydrogel dressings not only provide mechanical support for cell migration and proliferation, but also maintain a moist environment that facilitates skin wound healing (Zhao et al., 2023). Chondroitin sulfate (CS) is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan widely distributed in the ECM and cell surface of tissues. As natural polysaccharide, CS offers various biological functions such as certain antibacterial properties (Park et al., 2018) and exhibits good anti-inflammatory activity (Karakurt et al., 2019), rendering it an ideal material to prevent wound infection and stimulate wound healing. Nonetheless, pure CS hydrogel possesses relatively low mechanical strength and rapid degradation due to its low molecular weight (Farokhi et al., 2023; K. Y. Wang et al., 2023), which limits its clinical utility. Therefore, it is needed to combine CS with other biomaterials or ingredients to improve its mechanical properties and obtain a controllable degradation time.

Silk fibroin (SF) is a natural protein which has garnered attention in tissue engineering due to its excellent biocompatibility and mechanical properties (Goswami & Sen Sarma, 2023; K. Y. Wang et al., 2023). SF hydrogels could be prepared by ultrasound treatment or enzymic crosslinking, such as horseradish peroxidase (HRP)/hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) catalyzed reaction (Farokhi et al., 2021). Studies have indicated that SF hydrogels effectively promote re-epithelialization and angiogenesis in skin wounds (Luan et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2022). Chouhan et al. applied SF hydrogel to burn wounds and observed a tenfold increase in vascular density compared to the control group (Chouhan et al., 2018). Despite its promising application prospects in promoting skin wound healing, SF hydrogel still has shortcomings such as poor biodegradability and insufficient antibacterial properties. In this research, we combine SF with CS to fabricate a new hydrogel system for chronic wound management. SF enhances the mechanical strength of the hydrogel system by forming strong covalent bond networks. Meanwhile, it ensures a sustained release of CS as the hydrogel system gradually degrades, thereby imparting a prolonged therapeutic effect.

Infections can occur at any stage of wound healing, while antibiotics have been widely used for the treatment of bacterial infections, they come with many drawbacks such as low bioavailability and the emergence of drug resistance (Brugues & Needleman, 2014; Brukner & Oughton, 2020). Thus, antibiotic-free therapies were urgently needed such as photodynamic treatment (PDT) or photothermal therapy (PTT). Recently, PTT has attracted great attention due to its unique antibacterial mechanism and is expected to become an alternative to conventional antibiotic therapy (Bai et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2023). PTT could rapidly convert light energy into heat and cause irreversible thermal ablation of bacteria by disrupting cell homeostasis such as membrane damage or protein denaturation, leading to bacterial death at targeted sites (Che et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2023). Compared to traditional therapies, PTT offers the advantages of deep penetration, short treatment courses, fewer adverse effects, and most notably, the ability to address the challenge of drug resistance to classic antibiotic treatments (Hu et al., 2019). Nanoparticles with combined photothermal properties can be applied in PTT to enhance the infection-resistant characteristics of hydrogels, effectively eradicating bacteria through photothermal effects upon laser irradiation while minimizing the impact on surrounding healthy tissues (Y. K. Ren et al., 2022). Graphene oxide (GO), like many other carbon nanomaterials, exhibits a distinctive profusion of surface oxygen-functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups) which endows it with remarkable biocompatibility and the potential to promote vascular endothelial cell migration and neovascularization (Si et al., 2020; T. T. Wu et al., 2022). In addition, GO demonstrates substantial photothermal effects when subjected to near-infrared (NIR) irradiation, rendering it a promising candidate for photothermal antibacterial therapies (Geng et al., 2021; Lagos et al., 2022). Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are a novel type of graphene oxide derivatives that possess the inherent antibacterial and angiogenic activity of GO (Rojas-Andrade et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2023). Furthermore, the quantum dot-level sheet size (<100 nm) increases its surface area and the number of functional groups while reducing its dose-dependent toxicity and enhancing biocompatibility (Y. X. Ren et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2018). Therefore, this study aims to introduce GOQDs into the hydrogel system to harness their photothermal conversion properties and pro-angiogenic capabilities, imparting the hydrogel dressing with robust antibacterial and angiogenic properties.

In this study, we hypothesized that combining natural polysaccharide CS with natural protein SF would confer an adjustable degradation rate and improve the antibacterial properties of the hybrid hydrogel dressing. Further introducing GOQDs into composite hydrogels can enhance their antimicrobial and angiogenic properties. Based on the exceptional angiogenic activity of GOQDs@SF/CS (GSC) hydrogel, we further explored the formation of type H vessels and their role during the early stage of wound healing within the infected lesions. The multifunctional GSC hydrogel dressing with antibacterial properties through PTT and exceptional angiogenic activity was devised using an enzymatic crosslinking system. The composite GSC hydrogel dressing exhibits excellent photothermal antibacterial properties and biocompatibility in vitro. As type H vessels significantly increased, the related mechanisms were preliminarily explored in vitro. The antibacterial effect and angiogenic potential of the GSC hydrogel system exhibited the capacity to expedite the in vivo wound healing process in an infected full-thickness skin defect model. This strategy that GSC hydrogels resist bacteria via PTT and promotes type H vessel-related wound healing holds promise as an innovative avenue for addressing chronic wound management in clinical practice (Scheme 1 ).

Bombyx mori cocoons were purchased from Jiangsu Province, China. Chondroitin sulfate (CS, 95 %), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99.99 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99.99 %) and Luria-Bertani (LB) broth were purchased from Aladdin (China). Graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Nanjing/Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and 100 U/mL penicillin and streptomycin were purchased from HyClone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was ordered from Gibco (USA). All animal experiments were carried out in accordance with the National Research Council's Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals and were authorized by the animal experimentation ethical committee of the Ninth People's Hospital, affiliated with Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine (approval number: SH9H-2023-A820-1). Kunming (KM) mice (male, 6 weeks old) were bought from the Shanghai JieSiJie Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. (China).

SF was extracted from Bombyx mori cocoons according to a previously established method (Rajendra et al., 2022). First, 5 g cut cocoons were boiled in 1 L of 0.02 M Na2CO3 solution for 30 min and repeated three times to remove the sericin. After drying in an oven at 60 °C overnight, the degummed fibers were then dissolved in a ternary solution (molar ratio of CaCl2: H2O: ethanol = 1:8:2) at 100 °C. The obtained SF solution was dialyzed in deionized water for 3 days using a cellulose dialysis tube (molecular weight = 3500). Finally, the dialyzed SF solution was lyophilized for further use.

GOQDs were prepared as previously reported (Y. X. Ren et al., 2020). Briefly, 40 mg GO was added in 100 mL of deionized water and ultrasonication (40 kHz) was used for 30 min to achieve complete dispersion. Next, 40 mL sulfuric acid and 20 mL nitric acid were added into the solution and reacted at 70 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the acid was removed from the mixture by dialysis in deionized water for 3 days. Finally, the pure GOQDs were obtained by lyophilization and stored at −20 °C for further use.

The morphology of the obtained GOQDs was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos L120C, Thermo Scientific, USA). The topography and surface roughness of GOQDs were measured by atomic force microscope (AFM, Dimension ICON, Bruker, Germany), samples were dispersed in ultrapure water and then scanned at a speed of 12 μm/s. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific, USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI, Thermo Scientific, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD, Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) and Raman spectra (LabRAM HR Evolution, Horiba, Japan) were used corporately to analyze the structure of GOQDs. Besides, the particle size of GOQDs was investigated by using a particle size analyzer (Nicomp 380, PSS, USA).

The CS powder and lyophilized SF were dissolved in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH 7.4) to obtain a 10 wt% solution, respectively. SF solution was then mixed with H2O2 solution (final concentration 0.1 % v/v). CS solution was mixed with HRP solution (final concentration 200 U/mL) and different concentrations of GOQDs (final concentration 0.01, 0.05 and 0.1 mg/mL). The two obtained solutions above were mixed evenly at a 1:1 ratio and placed at 37 °C for gelation. Hydrogels containing different concentrations of GOQDs were denoted as SC, GSC1, GSC5, GSC10 (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of GSC hydrogels

ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogel samples were lyophilized in a freeze dryer. Then FTIR and XPS were used corporately to analyze the composition and structure of GSC hydrogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gelation time determination of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The time to form a gel (donated as the gelation time) was determined using the vial-tilting method (Cheng et al., 2023). Absence of flow within 15 s upon inverting the vial was regarded as the gel state. The experiments were performed in triplicate.

The rheological behavior of the hydrogels was analyzed using a rheometer (DHR-3, DE, USA) fitted with parallel plates (35 mm) with a 1 mm gap distance in the strain-controlled mode. All the experiments were conducted within the linear region at 37 °C. Oscillatory frequency sweeps were performed with an increasing oscillation frequency of 0.1–10 Hz at 0.1 % strain. The storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G") were recorded to analyze the gel-solid properties of the hydrogel.

The samples were lyophilized in a freeze dryer for 24 h to observe the surface microstructures and cross-sectional shapes of the hydrogels. The samples were then sprayed with gold and examined with a scanning electron microscope (SEM, S4800, Hitachi, Japan).

The hydrophilic properties of the hydrogel were characterized by a contact angle test. In order to measure the static contact angle, a 10 μL droplet was added to the surface of a customized platform with a pipette, and a side-view image was taken with the camera after the droplet had stabilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro swelling and degradation properties of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the swelling capacities, the hydrogel samples were pre-weighed and the weight was denoted as W0. Then, the samples were respectively incubated in PBS at 37 °C until swelling equilibrium. During the swollen process, the samples were taken out at the pre-determined time points and the residual liquid on the hydrogel surface was removed using filter papers, followed by a weight record of the samples as Wt. The swelling ratio was calculated with the following formula:

To determine the degradation characteristics, the hydrogel samples were immersed in PBS to reach swelling equilibrium. Then, the buffer was completely removed and replaced at different time points, the initial samples were weighed and recorded as W0. After removing the excess water on the surface of hydrogel, the samples were weighed again at different time points and the mass was recorded as Wt. The degradation ratio was defined with the following formula:

To explore the in vitro release profiles of GOQDs, GSC hydrogels were immersed in PBS within 1.5 mL vials at 37 °C. The emission spectra of GOQDs-containing supernatants at pre-determined time points (day 1, 3, 5, 7 and 14) were tested using an enzyme marker (Safire, TECAN, Switzerland) and the absorption peak readings were normalized to the control group (containing original concentration of GOQDs) to calculate the cumulative release percentage.

The mechanical properties of the hydrogels were evaluated by conducting the uniaxial tensile test and compression test using a universal testing machine (Shimadzu, Japan). For the compression test, the hydrogel was prepared into cylindrical samples (10 mm height and 10 mm diameter). Then, the compression tests were performed at a speed of 2 mm/min at room temperature. For the tensile test, the hydrogel was fabricated into a standard dumbbell model and the tensile tests were carried out with a speed of 10 mm/min until breaking point at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal performance measurements of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The photothermal performance of the hydrogel was evaluated by an 808 nm NIR laser. First, 100 μL GSC mixture with different concentrations of GOQDs (SC, GSC1, GSC5 and GSC10) was added into the Eppendorf tubes and left for 5 min to form a gel. After that, the GSC hydrogels were investigated by irradiating with an 808 nm NIR laser (2.0 W cm−2, 300 s). PBS was used as a control. The temperature change and NIR images were recorded by an infrared thermal imager (Fotric 280, China) at pre-set time. Then, different hydrogel components (SF; CS; GOQDs) were added to Eppendorf tubes to investigate their photothermal performance under laser irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 300 s), respectively. To further explore the influence of the NIR laser power density, the GSC10 hydrogel was irradiated by an 808 nm NIR laser for 300 s at different power densities (0.6, 1.3, 2.0 and 2.7 W cm−2). Besides, the photothermal stability and photothermal conversion efficiency of the hydrogel were explored by NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 300 s) for three cycles of healing-cooling processes. The photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of the hydrogel was calculated according to the following equation, based on a previous study (W. Z. Ren et al., 2015):

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial capability of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of GSC hydrogels against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC 29213) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC 25922) was investigated in vitro using the spread plate method. Briefly, the bacteria were cultured in LB broth overnight at 37 °C. 200 μL GSC solutions were added into 48-well plates for 5 min to get a gel state. Then, 200 μL bacteria suspension (1 × 104 CFU/mL) was spread evenly on the surface of the hydrogel and co-cultured for 6 h at 37 °C with or without NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 5 min). 100 μL bacterial solution was taken out from the well and diluted, then spread onto LB agar plates. After incubation at 37 °C overnight, the bacteria colonies on the plates were photographed and the number of CFUs was counted using Image J to evaluate the bacteria inhibitory effect. The bacteria suspension treated by PBS with or without NIR irradiation was served as the control group.

Live/Dead staining of bacteria was used to investigate the antibacterial properties of the hydrogel. After co-culture with different hydrogel samples for 6 h at 37 °C with or without NIR irradiation, the bacteria suspension was co-stained by DMAO and EthD-III (Yeasen, China) for 15 min in the dark according to the manufacturer's instructions. Finally, fluorescence images were captured using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica, Wetzlar, Germany).

Additionally, the morphology changes of bacteria were observed by SEM. Briefly, the bacteria were co-cultured on the surface of hydrogel samples for 6 h at 37 °C with NIR irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 5 min), then washed with PBS and fixed in 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at room temperature for 1 h. After fixation, the samples were dehydrated with gradient ethanol and observed by SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of the hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para: The L929 cells and Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) were used to study the cytotoxicity of the hydrogel. Briefly, the L929 cells and HUVECs were cocultured with different hydrogel using a transwell system, where the cells were seeded in the lower chamber of 24-well plates (5 × 104 cells per well) and the hydrogel samples were added into the upper chamber. After incubation at 37 °C under 5 % CO2 for 1, 3, and 5 days, the cells were stained with Calcein AM/PI dyes (Beyotime, China) for 30 min and observed under a fluorescent microscope (Ti-U, Nikon, Japan). Similarly, the cells were cocultured with different hydrogel in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well) using a transwell system and incubated at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. On day 1, 3 and 5, fresh DMEM containing 10 % CCK-8 solution (Dojindo, Japan) was added to each well and the absorbance of the solution was measured at 450 nm using an enzyme marker (Safire, TECAN, Switzerland) after incubation at 37 °C for 1 h.

L929 cells (5 × 104 cells per dish) were seeded on the hydrogel samples for 12 h. After cultivation, the samples were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min at room temperature. Then, the cytoskeletal arrangement was stained with FITC-phalloidin (Beyotime, China) for 1 h followed by nuclei staining with DAPI (Beyotime, China) for 10 min. Cell morphology of L929 cells on the hydrogel was observed using CLSM.

The hemocompatibility of GSC hydrogels was evaluated by in vitro hemolysis assay. Briefly, anticoagulated whole blood was acquired from mice. After centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 10 min, the erythrocytes were collected from the lower layer and then diluted with PBS to get a concentration of 5 % (v/v) erythrocyte stock solution. 500 μL hydrogel samples were prepared in the 48-well plate, followed by addition of 500 μL erythrocyte stock solution and placed on a shaker at 37 °C for 1 h. 500 μL 0.1 % Triton X-100 solution and PBS were regarded as the positive and negative controls. After incubation, the erythrocyte suspension was collected and centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min. The hemolysis property of the hydrogel was observed and photographed. Meanwhile, the absorbance value of the supernatant at 540 nm was measured using an enzyme marker (Safire, TECAN, Switzerland). The hemolysis ratio was calculated with the following formula:
where the AS represented the absorbance value of the sample supernatant; APC represented the absorbance value of the Triton X-100 positive control, and ANC represented the absorbance value of PBS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro angiogenesis capability of the hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para: The migration of HUVECs was studied using a 24-well transwell insert with a pore size of 8 μm (Corning, USA). The upper chamber was seeded with 100 μL HUVECs suspension (1 × 104 cells/well). Different hydrogel samples (200 μL) were added into the lower chamber with 500 μL culture medium as a chemoattractant. After 12 h of culture, the non-migrated cells in the upper chamber were carefully wiped off with a cotton swab. The migrated cells on the downward surface of the membrane were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min and stained with 1 % crystal violet for 30 min. Finally, the migrated HUVEVs were observed and photographed by a fluorescent microscope (Ti-U, Nikon, Japan).

To evaluate the effect of GSC hydrogels on endothelial migration, HUVECs were seeded in the lower chamber of a 24-well transwell plate (5 × 104 cells/well) and cultured with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C until they reached 90 % confluence. Then a sterile pipette tip (200 μL) was used to scratch a straight line in each well and the suspended cells were washed with PBS twice, followed by co-culture with 200 μL hydrogel samples in the upper chamber. At pre-determined time points (0,12,24 h), the cells were stained with Calcein dye (Beyotime, China) and observed under a fluorescence microscopy (Ti-U, Nikon, Japan). Scratch areas were calculated using Image J software.

The formation of capillary network was investigated by tube formation assay. Briefly, the Matrigel (354,230, Corning, USA) was melted at 4 °C overnight, then spread evenly on the bottom of 24-well plates (200 μL per well) and placed in an incubator (5 % CO2, 37 °C) for 30 min to form a gel. Afterward, HUVECs were seeded on the gel surface (1 × 105 cells/well) and incubated at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. The extracts of different hydrogels were used as the medium for HUVECs. 6 h later, the medium was removed and washed with PBS slightly. HUVECs, stained with Calcein dye (Beyotime, China), were observed using a fluorescent microscope (Ti-U, Nikon, Japan) and analyzed by the Angiotool software (v.0.6, National Institutes of Health, Maryland, USA).

Total RNA from HUVECs (6-well plates, 5 × 105 cells/well) after co-culture with the hydrogel for 48 h and skin tissues on day 7 were extracted by TRIzol Reagent (Takara Biomedical Technology Co., Ltd., Japan) according to the manufacturer's instructions. qRT-PCR was used to evaluate the relative RNA expression levels of the target gene. The primer sequences are listed in Table S2.

Total RNA was extracted from HUVECs with or without GSC10 hydrogel treatment (denoted as the GSC group and the control group, respectively) and subjected to standard RNA sequencing (RNA-Seq) using an Illumina Novaseq 6000 platform (LC-Bio Technology CO., Ltd., China) following the vendor recommended protocols. The global transcript profile of HUVECs was then compared between the GSC hydrogel group and the control group to determine the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) using pair-wise comparisons. The threshold was set as p < 0.05 and fold change cutoff of 2. The gene enrichment analysis was performed using the OmicStudio tools (https://www.omicstudio.cn/tool).

HUVECs were seeded in 6-well plates (5 × 105 cells/well) after co-culture with the hydrogel for 48 h and skin tissues on day 7 were lysed with RIPA lysis buffer (Beyotime, China) containing protease inhibitors and the supernatants were obtained by centrifugation. The protein concentration was determined by a BCA Protein Assay Kit. Then the supernatants were separated by SDS-PAGE gel electrophoresis and transferred to PVDF membranes (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA). Next, the membranes were blocked by 5 % BSA at room temperature and incubated with the primary antibodies at 4 °C overnight. The primary antibodies used in this study include antibodies against ERK1/2 (Proteintech, USA), p-ERK1/2 (Proteintech, USA), SLIT3 (Affinity, USA), CD31 (Proteintech, USA), Emcn (Affinity, USA), β-actin (Boster, USA). After incubation with the corresponding secondary antibodies for 1 h at room temperature, the membranes were washed with Tris-buffered saline-Tween (TBS-T, Solarbio, China) and visualized with an enhanced chemiluminescence detection system with exposure to FluorChem™ HD2 (Cell Biosciences, Inc., Santa Clara, CA, USA).

HUVECs were seeded in 6-well plates at a density of 5 × 105 cells/well with treatment of different hydrogels for 48 h using a transwell system. The supernatants were then collected and the level of SLIT3 protein was measured using a SLIT3 ELISA kit (Jiangsu Meimian Industrial Co., Ltd., China). All the operations were carried out according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Flow cytometric analysis was used to investigate the effect of GSC hydrogels on the differentiation of HUVECs toward type H vessel differentiation. HUVECs (5 × 105 cell/well) were seeded in the lower chamber of 6-well transwell plate and 400 μL GSC10 hydrogel was added into the upper chamber. After co-culture for 48 h, the cells were collected and subjected to immunostaining with antibodies. Finally, the HUVECs phenotype was analyzed by a flow cytometer (C6, BD, USA). Antibodies used in this assay include CD31 antibody (FAB3628G, R&D Systems, USA) and endomucin antibody (ab106100, Abcam, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operation of infected full-thickness skin defects
ce:section.ce:para: To access the in vivo wound healing performance of GSC hydrogels on mice. The full-thickness round skin defects with a diameter of ∼5 mm were symmetrically made on each side of the spine under intraperitoneal anesthesia. Then, the bacteria suspension of S. aureus (100 μL, 1 × 108 CFU/mL) was injected into the wound to establish an infected model. 24 h later, 16 mice were randomly divided into four groups and treated with different hydrogel samples, including PBS, SC, GSC10, GSC10 + NIR. The PTT (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 5 min) was conducted on day 1 and 3. After PTT on day 3, the infected tissue samples at the wound sites were retrieved and homogenized in a normal saline solution. Subsequently, 100 μL of the diluted bacterial suspension was aseptically spread onto the LB agar plate and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, followed by bacterial colonies counting and data analysis. At predetermined time points (day 0, 1, 3, 7 and 14), photographs of the wounds were obtained using a camera. Skin tissues around the wound site were harvested (day 7 and 14) and fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde for further analysis.

Skin specimens were fixed in paraformaldehyde for 2 days at room temperature, then embedded in paraffin and processed for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, Masson's trichrome staining, immunohistochemical staining (IL-6 and TNF-α). For immunofluorescence staining, skin sections were blocked in goat serum for 30 min at room temperature and incubated at 4 °C overnight with the corresponding primary antibodies: anti-CD31 (FAB3628G, R&D System, USA), anti-Emcn (DF13357, Affinity, USA). Next, they were incubated with corresponding secondary antibodies (Servicebio Co., Ltd., China) for 1 h and the cell nuclei were stained with DAPI (Beyotime, China) for 10 min in the dark. Finally, the immunofluorescence image was captured using CLSM.

After treatment with different hydrogel samples for 14 days, the fresh blood was collected from mice and processed for blood routine examinations and biochemistry analysis. Meanwhile, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) of mice were harvested and stained with H&E to evaluate the systemic toxicity of the hydrogel.

All studies were evaluated in at least three independent experiments. All data were presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) and analyzed via GraphPad Prism 9.0. The Student's t-test was used to determine significance between two groups and one- or two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used for multiple-group comparisons.

GOQDs possess outstanding antibacterial properties through physical interactions with bacterial membrane and PTT. In this study, uniform-size GOQDs were successfully synthesized. TEM analysis revealed the presence of GOQDs with a diameter of ∼30 nm (Fig. 1A). Furthermore, the HRTEM images clearly displayed lattice fringes of the GOQDs (Fig. S1), providing direct visual evidence of the synthesized GOQDs. The AFM 2D liner scanning showed the height of GOQDs was ∼2 nm (Fig. 1B) and the 3D reconstruction data confirmed its even distribution (Fig. 1C). The GOQDs showed three characteristic peaks of C-C/sp2C, CO, CO bond in the C1s peak fitting profile and the characteristic peaks of the CO and CO bond in the O1s peak fitting profile (Fig. S2 A-B). The diffraction peak of the GOQDs is centered at 2θ = 25.5° corresponding to the (002) plane (Fig. S2 C) and the Raman spectrum showed typical G and D peaks for graphene located at ∼1350 and ∼ 1570 cm−1 (Fig. S2 D), where the G peak is arisen from the sp2 hybridized structure of GOQDs, and the D peak is arisen from the chemical modification, defects and dislocation in GOQDs. These results validated the successful synthesis of GOQDs with scientific confidence (Kumar et al., 2017). The particle size analysis of GOQDs showed that most GOQDs exhibited diameters smaller than 50 nm (Fig. S3). The quantum dot-level size of GOQDs endows it with larger surface areas and better biocompatibility (Y. X. Ren et al., 2020; Tong et al., 2017), holding great promise for further applications in the biomedical field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the GSC hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The composite GSC hydrogel dressing was fabricated by simply incorporating different concentrations of GOQDs into the SF-CS hydrogel system with rapid crosslinking by HRP-catalyzed reaction (Fig. 1D). GSC hydrogels of different formulations were prepared as detailed in Table 1. The phenolic radicals generated by HRP/H2O2 reaction facilitate the formation of inter- or intrachain di-tyrosine bonds between the tyrosine residues of silk fibroin, resulting in a robust hydrogel with good elasticity (McGill et al., 2020). Gelation time analysis showed that all groups achieved gelation within ∼400 s. As GOQDs concentration increased to 0.05 mg/mL, the gelation time decreased to about 250 s (Table S1). This expedited gelation in the presence of GOQDs is likely due to their peroxidase-like catalytic activity (Sun et al., 2014), which accelerates the gelation process. The rheological behavior of the GSC hydrogels also showed that G' consistently surpassed G" with increasing oscillation frequency from 0.1 Hz to 10 Hz, indicating a stable gel state (Fig. S4).

The XPS elements profile of the GSC hydrogels is shown in Fig. 1E, apart from the characteristic peaks of carbon (284.96 eV) and oxygen (531.96 eV), the S2p peak (168.96 eV) was detected in the GSC10 and CS samples. Indicating the successful presence of CS in the composite hydrogel. Additionally, the FTIR spectra showed strong absorbance peaks at 1640 cm−1, 1520 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 (Fig. 1F), which were assigned to amide I, amide II and amide III, respectively. Among them, amide I is investigated to characterize the β-sheets, random coils and α-helices structure of SF. After crosslinking with HRP, no obvious difference was observed in GSC hydrogel at ∼1640 cm−1, suggesting that enzyme crosslinking resulted in only a minor structure transformation toward SF with good structural stability. Apart from the amide I band at 1640 cm−1, a strong absorbance peak of CS was also detected at 1245 cm−1, indicating the presence of the group O-SO3. Besides, a wide and strong band at ∼3400 cm−1 and an absorption band at ∼1100 cm−1 which demonstrated the stretching vibration of OH and CO group were observed in the GOQDs group and GSC group, indicating the successful incorporation of GOQDs into the composite hydrogel system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical characterizations of the GSC hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: SEM images of the hydrogel showed that the porous internal structures in all groups (Fig. 2A). Pore size analysis of the SEM images showed an average pore diameter of 100 μm (Fig. 2B), which is suitable for cell attachment, infiltration and growth (Abbasi et al., 2020). The water contact angle tests demonstrated a contact angle below 50° in all groups (Fig. 2C and D), which indicated excellent hydrophilicity of the composite hydrogel system (M. Q. Wang et al., 2013). As GOQDs concentration increased, the contact angles significantly decreased, with GSC10 exhibiting the most significant reduction compared to the other groups. This behavior is likely due to the presence of a greater number of hydrophilic groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy groups) on the surface of GOQDs which enhanced the hydrophilicity of the GSC hydrogels, conducive to better biocompatibility and cell attachment.

Swelling and degradation properties of the hydrogel were assessed in this study to determine its fluid-absorbing capability and bio-compatibility. All hydrogel samples reached equilibrium after immersion for ∼6 h (Fig. 2E). Furthermore, the GSC10 hydrogel exhibited a lower swelling ratio (∼60 %) compared to other groups, especially the SC group (∼80 %). In vitro degradation test demonstrated ∼60 % degradation ratio in all groups over a 14-day monitoring period (Fig. 2F), which is significantly higher than that of the pure SF hydrogel (Fig. S5). Notably, the GSC10 group showed a slightly lower degradation ratio than that of other groups after 14 days' immersion in PBS (51.13 % on day 14). The relatively low swelling and degradation ratio could be in favor of maintaining the dynamic wound environment, reducing the risk of maceration and, at the same time, ensuring the controlled medication release over an extended treatment period (Zhan et al., 2021; L. W. Zhang et al., 2022).

Mechanical properties and stability are also urgently addressed in hydrogel dressings, especially on dynamic wounds. Stress-strain curves of the GSC hydrogels were studied. All hydrogels prolonged up to ∼50 % of the original length in the tensile test (Fig. 2G). However, the GSC10 hydrogel exhibited the highest Young's modulus (81.35 kPa) compared with other groups (Fig. 2H). Similar results were observed in the compression test (Fig. 2I and J) where the GSC10 hydrogel surpassed other groups with a compression modulus of 136.23 kPa. The results indicated that the mechanical properties of the hydrogel were strengthened when GOQDs concentration increased, with GSC10 exhibiting the largest mechanical strength. This improvement is likely due to more effective gel crosslinking facilitated by the incorporation of GOQDs. The incorporated GOQDs served as both physical and chemical cross-linkers, which facilitated the formation of both interfacial hydrogen bonds and a covalent network that interconnected molecular chains with varying lengths, providing the material with enhanced suitability for the dynamic and intricate wound healing environment (Du et al., 2022; S. W. Wu et al., 2017). Overall, the incorporation of GOQDs into the hydrogel matrix has a significant impact on the hydrogels' structural, physical, and mechanical properties, suggesting their potential utility in biomedical applications, particularly as wound dressings in chronic skin management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal performance of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the in vitro photothermal performance of the GSC hydrogels, the 808 nm NIR laser was chosen as light source because of its low light absorption coefficient of hemoglobin and water, minimizing the impact of laser irradiation on normal tissues (Liu et al., 2016). We firstly examined the photothermal performance of the GSC hydrogels with different concentrations of GOQDs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the temperature of GSC10 hydrogel increased rapidly after NIR laser irradiation (2.0 W cm−2), reaching 40 °C within 2 min and maintaining it at around 50 °C. On the contrary, the temperature of other groups remained under 40 °C during the whole process. The temperature of the PBS and SC group remained nearly constant (∼25 °C). The temperature-time curve further confirmed the excellent photothermal performance of GSC10 hydrogel with a maximum temperature of 51 °C (Fig. 3B). Next, to validate the key role of GOQDs in the photothermal conversion, we assessed the photothermal performance of each component of the composite hydrogel. The results indicated that PBS, SF, and CS showed minimal temperature changes under NIR irradiation (2.0 W cm−2, 5 min). While the GOQDs exhibited a remarkable temperature increase and ultimately maintained at ∼45 °C (Fig. S6). To determine the influence of NIR laser power density, we assessed the temperature changes in GSC hydrogels at different power densities. As expected, increasing the power density led to higher hydrogel temperatures. At 2.0 W cm−2, the hydrogel maintained a temperature of 51 °C after 5 min of NIR irradiation (Fig. 3C), which is high enough to kill bacteria and leave a negligible impact on normal tissues (Zhao et al., 2023). Though the temperature at 2.7 W cm−2 demonstrated a higher temperature at the same time point (>60 °C), the power density of 2.0 W cm−2 was chosen to balance effective photothermal sterilization while minimizing damage to surrounding tissues (Guo et al., 2022).

Next, the photothermal conversion efficiency of the GSC10 hydrogel was investigated based on its temperature profile during a single heating-cooling cycle. The hydrogel exhibited an impressive photothermal conversion efficiency of 48.49 % (Fig. 3D), which is higher than many photothermal agents reported previously (F. P. Li et al., 2022; Qi et al., 2022; Z. X. Wang et al., 2022), indicating its excellent photothermal performance. The photothermal stability of the hydrogel was investigated by three on-off NIR irradiation cycles. The hydrogel maintained its high-temperature levels (∼51 °C) consistently across the cycles (Fig. 3E), demonstrating its remarkable photothermal stability. These findings collectively highlight the exceptional in vitro photothermal performance of the GSC hydrogels, with the ability to achieve and maintain high temperatures efficiently, positioning it as a promising candidate for photothermal antibacterial therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial capability of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding photothermal properties of GSC hydrogels inspired us to delve into its antibacterial activity in vitro. As shown in Fig. 4A, an increase in GOQDs concentration was observed to result in a slight reduction in bacterial colonies without NIR irradiation. This hints at the inherent antibacterial attributes of GOQDs, possibly due to their physiochemical interactions that disrupt bacterial membranes and result in pathogen inactivation (Nichols & Chen, 2020; Zheng et al., 2018). Moreover, a significant reduction in bacterial colonies of E. coli and S. aureus could be observed under NIR irradiation (2.0 W cm−2, 300 s), particularly in the GSC10 group with nearly no sight of bacterial colonies. The bacterial survival ratios were in alignment with the plate counting experiments (Fig. 4B and C). The GSC10 group exhibited an antibacterial effect with a bacterial survival ratio of 74.4 % for E. coli and 51.0 % for S. aureus. After NIR laser irradiation treatment (2.0 W cm−2, 300 s), this antibacterial effect was significantly enhanced with a bacterial survival ratio of nearly 0 % for both E. coli and S. aureus. For further examination of the bacterial viability, fluorescent live/dead staining for bacteria was conducted where live bacteria emitted green fluorescence and dead bacteria emitted red fluorescence. As shown in the fluorescence images (Fig. 4D), most of the E. coli and S. aureus stayed alive in the control and SC groups, indicating a negligible inhibitory effect on bacterial growth. In contrast, more dead bacteria with red fluorescence appeared with increasing concentrations of GOQDs, especially in the GSC10 group. In particular, almost all E. coli and S. aureus were eradicated after PTT for 300 s. Few bacteria were seen in the GSC5 group and no evidence of live bacteria was observed in the GSC10 group. The live/dead fluorescence ratios (Fig. 4E and F) further reiterated the impressive antibacterial effects of GSC10 hydrogel with no sign of either live E. coli or live S. aureus after PTT treatment, corroborating our previous findings.

Additionally, SEM was employed to gain insights into the morphological changes of E. coli and S. aureus following NIR irradiation (2.0 W cm−2, 300 s). As shown in Fig. 4G, bacteria retained their regular morphology in the control and SC groups. However, with increasing GOQDs concentration, the enhanced photothermal effect led to noticeable bacterial deformations, membrane rupture and cell shrinkage. GSC10 group with NIR irradiation demonstrated the most severe bacterial deformations, illustrating the hydrogel's efficacy in photothermal bacterial elimination. These results underscored the significant potential of the GSC hydrogels in achieving efficient bacterial sterilization. The inspiring results demonstrated the potential of the composite hydrogel to be utilized in infected wound management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biocompatibility of the hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The biocompatibility of GSC hydrogels was evaluated with a series of experiments in vitro with L929 cells and HUVECs. The live/dead cell staining results showed almost no cell death was observed in all groups after co-culture with the GSC hydrogels for 1, 3 and 5 days (Fig. 5A and B), demonstrating minimal cytotoxicity of the hydrogel. Besides, there were more live cells in the GSC10 group, especially on day 5. The CCK-8 assay was conducted to further investigate the cell proliferation and the results showed a similar trend. On day 3 and 5, the GSC10 group displayed relatively higher OD values, with a significant difference observed when compared to the control and GSC5 group on day 5 (Fig. 5C and D). The corresponding cell viability was calculated at each time point and normalized to the control group. Notably, the cell viability of GSC10 group could reach ∼120 % (Fig. S7), demonstrating a great pro-proliferative effect of the GSC10 hydrogel which surpasses the similar formulations composed of pure SF/CS hydrogel as previously reported (Farokhi et al., 2023). Cell adhesion not only influences cell behavior but also lays the foundation for further biological responses, such as proliferation, migration, and differentiation. Cytoskeletal staining of L929 cells was performed to further investigate cell adhesion behavior on the hydrogel surfaces. The fluorescence images portrayed the most excellent spreading on the GSC hydrogel surface, compared to other groups (Fig. S8). Notably, more spindle-shaped cells were observed in the SC group than in the pure SF group, which could be attributed to the promoting effects of CS on cell adhesion.

The blood compatibility of the hydrogel was also examined through a hemolysis assay. All hydrogel groups exhibited minimal hemolysis, with hemolysis rates consistently below 5 % (Fig. 5E). These results demonstrated the high compatibility of the hydrogel materials with blood components. In essence, the cumulative experiments have emphasized the exceptional biocompatibility of the composite GSC hydrogels when examined in vitro. This distinctive attribute establishes our hydrogel as a secure and auspicious contender for subsequent in vivo applications.

Transwell assay was conducted to evaluate effects of GSC hydrogels on cell migration. The GSC10 hydrogel group exhibited the most migrated cells than other groups after incubation for 12 h (Fig. 6A). The statistical analysis results showed a significant difference between the GSC10 group and other groups (Fig. 6B), showcasing its capacity to significantly promote cell migration. Additionally, the scratch assay demonstrated that with increasing concentrations of GOQDs, the scratching area progressively reduced. At 24 h, the scratching area in GSC10 group almost recovered totally (Fig. 6C). Percentage of scratching areas showed that the GSC group possessed the smallest areas all the time and even demonstrated a significant difference compared with the GSC5 group at 24 h (Fig. 6D and E). This consistency further confirmed the GSC hydrogels' effective facilitation of cell motility. Furthermore, the results of transwell assay and scratch assay showed that SC hydrogel treatment provided more migrated cells and smaller scratching areas than the pure SF hydrogel treatment (Fig. S9), indicating the promoting effect of chondroitin sulfate on cell migration.

Tube formation assay was conducted to examine the influence of the GSC hydrogels on vessel formation in HUVECs. As shown in the microscopic images (Fig. 6F), the control group displayed a few tube formation, the SC and GSC1 group exhibited limited tube formation, while the GSC5 and GSC10 group demonstrated significantly more tube formation with more junctions and longer vessel length observed in the GSC10 group. The quantitative analysis of the number of junctions and total vessel length demonstrated great significance between GSC10 and other groups (Fig. 6G and H), indicating its potential in promotion of vascularization. Compared to previously reported studies with similar formulations (Farokhi et al., 2023; Meng et al., 2023), our composite hydrogel demonstrated smaller scratching areas and promoting effect of vascularization, indicating a potential of accelerating wound healing through neo-angiogenesis. Based on these in vitro experiment results, the GSC10 hydrogel demonstrated superior antibacterial activity, better biocompatibility and promoting effects of cell adhesion and angiogenesis. Thus, we chose the GSC10 hydrogel to explore the release performance of GOQDs and utilized this hydrogel group in further animal experiments.

The fluorescence emission spectroscopy of GOQDs indicated a sustained and stable release of GOQDs within the hydrogel system, without any significant burst release observed (Fig. S10). The GSC10 hydrogel showed a cumulative release of GOQDs at ∼6 % in 24 h and ∼ 54 % in 14 days (Fig. S11). The release of GOQDs is attributed to both swelling and degradation of the composite hydrogel. During swelling, the internal network structure was enlarged by water absorption, leading to the increased pore size. Consequently, the free GOQDs in the hydrogel pores could get out through diffusion. Furthermore, with hydrogel degrading stepwise, the internal network structure broke down, resulting that the majority of embedded GOQDs released from the hydrogel (Kharkar et al., 2014; Lei et al., 2022; Vigata et al., 2020).

Type H vessels, characterized by high expression levels of both CD31 and Emcn (CD31hi Emcnhi), have been reported to play an essential role in the healing process of skin wounds, especially in the proliferation phase (Lin et al., 2023). To determine the potential effect of the GSC hydrogels on the endothelial cell differentiation toward this specific vessel phenotype, the expression levels of its two markers, CD31 and Emcn, were investigated in HUVECs after co-culture with different hydrogels for 48 h. The PCR results revealed that with increasing concentrations of GOQDs, there was a corresponding increase in the mRNA expression levels of CD31 and Emcn (Fig. 6I and J). Similar to the Transwell and scratch assays, the GSC10 group demonstrated the highest expression levels of the two key markers. This result indicated the possible existence of CD31hi Emcnhi endothelial cells induced by the GSC hydrogels. The flow cytometry results showed that more CD31hi Emcnhi endothelial cells appeared in the GSC10 group (1.07 %) than in the control group (0.24 %) (Fig. S12), further confirming the presence of CD31hi Emcnhi endothelial cells induced by GSC hydrogel treatments. Collectively, these in vitro experiments showed that the GSC hydrogels promoted the migration of endothelial cells and their differentiation toward a new vessel subtype—type H vessels. However, the molecular mechanism by which GSC hydrogels promote the generation of type H vessels remains unclear.

To explore the possible molecular mechanism in the formation of type H vessels induced by GSC hydrogels, RNA-Seq analysis was conducted on HUVECs treated with the GSC10 hydrogel. As shown in the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) plot (Fig. 7A), the transcriptional profile of the GSC10 group and the control group showed distinctive distributions. We then compared the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) by setting a threshold at p < 0.05 and fold change (FC) > 2 (Fig. 7B). Among the total 34,728 DEGs, there were 169 up-regulated genes and 9 down-regulated genes in the GSC group. The results of Gene Ontology enrichment analysis further explored the DEGs mainly involved in immune response, signal transduction, and positive regulation of extracellular signal-regulated kinase (ERK)1/2 cascade, which has great significance compared to the control group (Fig. 7C and S13, 14). The Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) based on Gene Ontology and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) confirmed the positive regulation of ERK1/2 cascade (Fig. 7D) and the up-regulation of mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signaling pathway (Fig. S15). These results demonstrated that ERK1/2 was significantly activated in HUVECs by the GSC10 hydrogel, which is consistent with other reports that focused on the gene expression changes in cells treated with graphene and its derivatives (Kang et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2023). ERK1/2 is a member of the MAPK family and the phosphorylation of ERK1/2 plays a pivotal role in the transduction of cell growth or differentiation-related signals into the nucleus from cytoplasm. Many studies have indicated a close correlation between the phosphorylation of ERK1/2 and the angiogenic potential of endothelial cells, with increased phosphorylation of ERK1/2 promoting endothelial cell proliferation and migration (Song et al., 2023). Thus, we speculate that the GSC hydrogels may achieves pro-angiogenic effects by activating ERK1/2 signaling pathway.

SLIT3 is a secreted repulsive axon guidance molecule which is capable of stimulating the proliferation, motility and vascular network formation of endothelial cells, acting as a mitogen and chemoattractant for endothelial cells (B. Zhang et al., 2009). Recently, Li et al. have invented a hydrogel microparticles system loaded with SLIT3. Their findings demonstrated that the SLIT3-loaded hydrogel could enhance the formation of type H vessels and accelerate the healing process in mice model, indicating a pivotal role of SLIT3 in the neovascularization (J. Li et al., 2023). Based on results in Fig. 6I and J that the GSC hydrogels promoted the differentiation toward type H vessels, the expression of SLIT3 was further explored. As showed in Fig. S16 A-B, the GSC10 group exhibited the highest mRNA expression of SLIT3 and more protein secretion in the supernatants, indicating an essential role of SLIT3 in the type H vessel formation induced by GSC hydrogels.

These inspiring results showed the involvement of ERK1/2 and SLIT3 in the angiogenesis process. WB results showed an increase in CD31, Emcn, SLIT3 and phosphorylated ERK1/2 (p-ERK1/2) protein expression levels with rising GOQDs concentrations (Fig. 7E), which preliminarily verify our hypothesis that GSC hydrogels promotes angiogenesis and H-type angiogenesis by activating ERK1/2 signaling pathway and upregulating the expression of SLIT3.

Studies have demonstrated that Schnurri-3 (SHN3) downregulated the expression of SLIT3 by suppressing ERK1/2 activity (Xu et al., 2018). Knockdown of ERK1/2 interacting motif in SHN3(Shn3KI/KI mice) displayed higher level of SLIT3 and lead to increased formation of type H vessels (Xu et al., 2018). Silencing ERK1/2 in osteoblasts could also lead to decreased expression of SLIT3 and inhibit the formation of type H vessels (Yallowitz et al., 2023). Taken together, in this research the GSC hydrogels possibly activated ERK1/2 signaling pathway to stimulate the transcription and translation of SLIT3, ultimately leading to more CD31hi Emcnhi endothelial cells for type H vessel formation. However, the specific relationship between ERK1/2 signaling pathway and expression of SLIT3 is unclear, which still needs further exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo infected wound healing of the GSC hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo healing potential of GSC hydrogels was investigated in an infected full-thickness skin defect model (Fig. 8A). Based on our previous results, the GSC10 hydrogel was chosen for in vivo experiments due to its outstanding antibacterial and pro-angiogenic abilities. The real-time thermal images of the GSC10 + NIR group under NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 2.0 W cm−2, 5 min) revealed that the temperature at the wound site reached 42.4 °C after 2 min of NIR irradiation and 51.1 °C after 5 min of NIR irradiation (Fig. S17). It's reported that above 50 °C, the enzymes in bacteria become denatured and their proteins and lipids on the cell membranes are damaged, eventually causing bacterial death (Hsiao et al., 2015; Huo et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2023; M. C. Wu et al., 2013). Besides, some materials such as AuNCs could speedily heat up with NIR showing an outstanding PTT (Yougbaré et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2023). However, their over high temperature of 60–70 °C after irradiation possesses the risk of burning damage to cells and tissues, which is not suitable for wound repair. The temperature of our developed GSC10 hydrogel after continuous NIR treatment would not exceed 55 °C. Thus, GSC10 hydrogel not only exerts the antibacterial function of PTT, but also achieves biosafety temperature control, which has great potential for clinical application. Photographs captured at various time points (day 0, 1, 3, 7, and 14) and wound traces extracted from these images provided visual evidence of the changes in wound appearance (Fig. 8B and C). The wound areas gradually decreased over time in all groups. However, on day 7, the GSC10 + NIR group demonstrated a relatively smaller wound area compared to other groups. Moreover, the wound of GSC10 + NIR group almost completely healed on day 14, while those in other groups took longer to recover. Besides, the wound closure ratios were calculated and the percentages of wound closure in the PBS group, the SC group, the GSC10 group and the GSC10 + NIR group were 58.9 %, 59.6 %, 64.8 % and 73.1 % on day 3. Then on day 7, their wound closure ratios increased to 74.9 %, 78.7 %, 89.6 % and 97.7 %, and finally reached 89.7 %, 94.0 %, 96.9 % and 99.2 % on day 14, respectively (Fig. 8D). The GSC10 + NIR group demonstrated an obviously higher wound closure than the similar formulations (∼90 %) (Tang et al., 2019). These results demonstrated the excellent therapeutic effect of the GSC10 hydrogel under NIR irradiation, the accelerated healing rate of which could be attributed to its photothermal antibacterial activity and pro-angiogenic ability.

To quantify the wound infection conditions, bacterial colonization within the skin tissue was assessed on day 3. The results showed that lower bacterial counts were observed in both the GSC10 and GSC10 + NIR groups than those in the PBS and SC groups. Importantly, the GSC10 + NIR group displayed the most significant reduction in bacterial colonies, with nearly no colonies detected (Fig. 8E). The bacterial survival ratios of the SC group, the GSC10 group and the GSC10 + NIR group were 96.9 %, 53.3 %, and 2.6 %, respectively (Fig. 8F). Our study suggested that the GSC hydrogels, especially when combined with NIR irradiation, possesses notable antibacterial properties and promotes the healing of infected wounds in vivo. If the infection of the wound is not controlled, the tissue regeneration would be severely hindered and the wound would be unable to heal (Bal-Öztürk et al., 2021; Vivcharenko et al., 2023). With the improved antibacterial capabilities attributed to GOQDs incorporation, GSC hydrogels could directly combat pathogenic microorganisms in the wounds, thus preventing infection aggravation and rebuilding a healing environment. Additionally, the evaluation of systemic toxicity through monitoring the animals' weight revealed no significant differences among the groups (Fig. 8G), indicating the in vivo safety profile of the hydrogel.

Furthermore, histopathological staining was performed to evaluate the details of the wound healing process. As shown in the H&E staining results (Fig. 9A), the GSC10 + NIR group displayed significantly reduced wound widths with homogeneous epidermis on day 7. In the SC and PBS groups, however, the wounds were much wider with incomplete epidermis. And on day 14, all groups underwent near-complete restoration. Moreover, we found that GSC10 hydrogel degraded completely in vivo within 14 days, which was rather different with that only ∼54 % of GOQDs release from the GSC10 hydrogel after 14 days in vitro (Fig. S11). Actually, hydrogels degrade more rapidly in vivo than in vitro due to the presence of enzymes and endocytosis (Mazzeo et al., 2019; L. Wang et al., 2017). The released GOQDs were possibly transported into the plasma through endocytosis and then enhanced cell proliferation as well as vascularization. The quantification of wound width and epidermal thickness also aligned with these findings (Fig. 9B, C and S18). Notably, the epidermal thickness of GSC10 + NIR group was the thickest among all the groups on day 7, and decreased to the levels of normal skin on day 14. By contrast, the PBS group showed thin and discontinuous epidermis on day 7 and remained thick epidermis by day 14. This phenomenon could be attributed to the delayed wound healing caused by infection, and demonstrating the great advantage of utilizing GSC hydrogel dressings in infected wounds.

Moreover, we observed denser collagen deposition with more organized structures in the GSC10 + NIR group on day 7 and 14 (Fig. 9D, E and S19). The data analysis of collagen deposition also showed a significant difference between GSC10 + NIR and other groups. Immunohistochemical staining targeting inflammatory markers TNF-α and IL-6 and the corresponding quantitative analysis demonstrated that both GSC10 and GSC10 + NIR groups exhibited reduced inflammation levels (Fig. 9F-H), with the latter exhibiting the most significant anti-inflammatory effects. Collectively, these findings underscored the pivotal role of GSC hydrogels in fostering rapid wound healing, collagen deposition enhancement, and inflammation reduction under NIR laser irradiation.

The immunofluorescence staining results of CD31 and Emcn revealed that the GSC10 and GSC10 + NIR group exhibited significantly higher expression levels of CD31 and Emcn on day 7, and the merged images exhibited the characteristic presence of CD31hi Emcnhi type H vessels (Fig. 10A). in the PBS and SC group, however, nearly no evidence of CD31 or Emcn was observed but nuclei, indicating a lack of neovascularization. Particularly, the GSC10 + NIR group demonstrated superior vascular tissue, potentially owing to the localized thermal effects mediated by NIR irradiation and the bacteria-free microenvironment, which facilitated neovascularization and tissue regeneration (Sheng et al., 2021; X. Zhang et al., 2021). These results showed that the GSC10 hydrogel promoted type H vessel formation at the early stage of wound healing and played a key role in the neovascularization and re-epithelization, and this therapeutic effect could be enhanced when combined with PTT. Furthermore, the mRNA expression levels of CD31 and Emcn in the tissues on day 7 were also consistent with the immunofluorescence findings, showing a substantial increase in the expression of type H vessel markers in the GSC10 + NIR group (Fig. 10B and C). The mRNA levels of SLIT3 also exhibited significant differences compared to other groups, aligning with the cellular-level validation results (Fig. 10D). The WB results showed an increase in CD31, Emcn, and SLIT3 protein levels in the GSC10 and GSC10 + NIR groups, alongside a significant elevation in p-ERK1/2 expression (Fig. 10E). These findings aligned with our previous findings in vitro, suggesting that the GSC hydrogels promoted type H vessel formation by activating ERK1/2 signaling pathway and upregulating the expression of SLIT3.

Finally, histopathological examination of major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, was conducted to validate the preclinical safety of the hydrogel by using the H&E staining on day 14. The results revealed no significant differences or pathological changes among the groups (Fig. S20), signifying the excellent histocompatibility of the composite hydrogel with major organ tissues. Additionally, the hematological and blood biochemical analyses indicated a lack of significant variations or abnormalities among the groups (Fig. S21), suggesting that the composite hydrogel did not induce any systemic hematological issues or adverse blood biochemical changes. These findings collectively establish the robust in vivo biocompatibility of the GSC hydrogels, underscoring their safety for potential clinical applications and their promising suitability for a wide range of biomedical and therapeutic uses.

In this study, the GSC hydrogels were successfully developed with excellent biocompatibility and biosafety profile, which hold immense promise as innovative wound dressings for bacteria elimination and infected wound healing. The component of CS could elevate the wound healing effect of the GSC composite hydrogels by the improved biodegradation, cell adhesion and migration. The incorporation of GOQDs has not only bolstered the mechanical properties of the hydrogel, enhancing its suitability for dynamic wound management, but also endowed the GSC hydrogels with photothermal capability for effectively eradicating bacteria via PTT that presents a promising alternative to traditional antibiotic-based approaches, addressing concerns related to antibiotic resistance and offering a potential solution to this pressing issue. Moreover, GSC hydrogels hold the capacity to promote endothelial cell migration and facilitate the generation of type H vessels in wound healing process via activating ERK1/2 signaling pathway and upregulating SLIT3 both in vitro and in vivo. In summary, the GSC hydrogels emerge as a versatile and comprehensive solution for clinical bacteria-infected wound healing.
